coredata.dc:title: Organic amine-grafted carbon quantum dots with tailored surface and enhanced photoluminescence properties
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Mingyu
Hu, Chao
Yu, Chang
Wang, Shuai
Zhang, Peng
Qiu, Jieshan
coredata.dc:description: A facile one-step synthesis strategy has been developed for producing fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from a kind of Chinese coal, Jincheng anthracite. Surface grafting of the CQDs was completed and systematically investigated by grafting with a series of organic amino molecules. It was found that the solubility and the optical properties of CQDs vary with and depend on its surface chemistry to some degree. Of organic amine-grafted coal-derived CQDs available now, ethylenediamine-grafted CQDs (EDA-CQDs) possess an obviously enhanced photoluminescence (PL) with the quantum yield (QY) as high as 18.6%, being an increase of 24.5 times in comparison with that of the pristine CQDs (0.73%), and have a capability of well dispersing within other polymer matrixes due to their good compatibility after amine-grafting. The hybrid monolith made of amine-grafted CQDs and polymer gelatin exhibits excellent PL properties even at a relatively high concentration of CQDs, indicative of great potential applications in various optical materials and devices.

U1203292 and 21336001 ), the Education Department of Liaoning Province of China ( T2013001 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( DUT14LAB06 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram for the surface grafting of CQDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) SEM image of Jincheng anthracite. (b) XRD patterns for Jincheng anthracite and as-obtained CQDs. (c) TEM image of CQDs. The inset is the high-resolution TEM of the CQDs. Scale bar, 2nm. (d) Size distribution of CQDs. The histograms of CQDs size distributions were obtained by counting 200 particles of the sample. The Gauss fit curve is colored in black.
UV–vis spectrum and PL spectra of the as-obtained CQDs.
FTIR spectra of as-prepared CQDs (a), EDA-CQDs (b), BDA-CQDs (c), HMDA-CQDs (d), DDA-CQDs (e) and ODA-CQDs (f).
PL emission spectra (excited at 360nm) of the original CQDs and grafted CQDs.

PL emission spectra of the as-obtained CQDs and different amine-grafted CQDs at different excitation wavelengths as indicated. Inset: normalized PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. (a) as-obtained CQDs, (b) EDA-CQDs, (c) BDA-CQDs, (d) HMDA-CQDs, (e) DDA-CQDs, (f) ODA-CQDs.
Digital photographs of the gelatin-CQDs composites under visible (a) and UV light (b) (The sample bottles were upside down.) (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparison of QY between the original CQDs and grafted CQDs.

head.ce:title: Organic amine-grafted carbon quantum dots with tailored surface and enhanced photoluminescence properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Carbon Research Laboratory, Liaoning Key Lab for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, State Key Lab of Fine Chemicals, School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Key Lab for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering
State Key Lab of Fine Chemicals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile one-step synthesis strategy has been developed for producing fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from a kind of Chinese coal, Jincheng anthracite. Surface grafting of the CQDs was completed and systematically investigated by grafting with a series of organic amino molecules. It was found that the solubility and the optical properties of CQDs vary with and depend on its surface chemistry to some degree. Of organic amine-grafted coal-derived CQDs available now, ethylenediamine-grafted CQDs (EDA-CQDs) possess an obviously enhanced photoluminescence (PL) with the quantum yield (QY) as high as 18.6%, being an increase of 24.5 times in comparison with that of the pristine CQDs (0.73%), and have a capability of well dispersing within other polymer matrixes due to their good compatibility after amine-grafting. The hybrid monolith made of amine-grafted CQDs and polymer gelatin exhibits excellent PL properties even at a relatively high concentration of CQDs, indicative of great potential applications in various optical materials and devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots, as an emerging new class of fluorescent nanomaterials, have drawn an increasing attention due to their high photostability against photobleaching, tunable excitation and emission wavelengths, good biocompatibility and low molecular weight in comparison with semiconductor quantum dots and other fluorescent nanoparticles [1–4]. So far, various methods have been explored to prepare CQDs, such as electrochemical synthesis [5–9], acidic oxidation [10,11], supported synthesis [12,13], microwave assisted methods [14–16], ultrasonic synthesis [17], arc discharge [18] and laser ablation [1,19–22]. Among these methods available now, chemical oxidation with strong acids or oxidants has become a popular strategy, in which high-quality CQDs can be prepared at low cost and large-scale production. Besides, a series of carbon sources, including graphite oxide [14], carbon nanotubes [23], ammonium carboxylate salts [24], carbon soot from natural gas [25] and burning candles [11] and carbohydrates [15] have been used to prepare CQDs. It was found that, coal, as a kind of abundant and affordable carbon source, has demonstrated a charming superiority in comparison with other carbon sources [26]. In addition, although various methods and carbon sources have been investigated in the preparation of CQDs, the CQDs with a high quantum yield (QY) are still sought after and highly desirable. It is believed that surface grafting with organic agents is a feasible strategy to improve and tailor the PL properties of CQDs and provide an important method for modification of fluorescent nanoparticles [27–30]. Gogotsi and his co-workers have successfully reported the synthesis of fluorescent nanodiamond by covalent linking of octadecylamine to the surface of nanodiamond. The surface-modified nanodiamond shows a remarkably blue fluorescence and enhanced dispersion property [28]. However, the influences of various grafting agents on the properties of coal-based CQDs and their sensitivity to organic amino agents have never been explored systematically.

Herein, we first synthesized fluorescent CQDs from Jincheng anthracite via an inexpensive and facile one-step wet chemistry route. Besides, the as-obtained CQDs were grafted with various organic amino molecules. The influences of the amino groups with different lengths of the hydrocarbon chains on the PL properties of the coal-based CQDs were systematically investigated and compared. Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic of the surface grafting process, which was carried out by refluxing CQDs in the presence of sulfoxide chloride, followed by stirring with different amides under N2 protection to link the amino group on the edge of CQDs. The modified CQDs demonstrate enhanced PL properties with high QY, and its surface hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity can be tuned and controlled due to the existence of surface amino groups, leading to controllable stabilization of CQDs in the water or organic phase. CQDs/gelatin hybrid monoliths can be produced by homogeneously dispersing amine-grafting CQDs in gelatin. It is believed that such unique characteristics will provide potential possibilities for various optical materials and devices [29,31].

The carbon source is anthracite from Jincheng, China. Its analysis data are shown in Table S1. The coal sample without pretreatment was crushed and sieved to 74μm, and fully dried before use. Dialysis bags (molecular weight cut off=3500Da) were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Concentrated nitric acid (65–68wt.%) and sodium hydroxide were ordered from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory of China. Gelatin was bought from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Reagent Factory of China.

Pristine CQDs were prepared by chemical oxidation and etching from the Jincheng anthracite, which had been reported in our previous work [32]. For a typical run, 120mg coal was dispersed in a 30mL of concentrated nitric acid under ultrasonication for 1h, keeping stirring at 140°C for 24h. The reaction system was then cooled down to room temperature, followed by continuously dropping sodium hydroxide into the above solution to turn it to neutrality, yielding a supernatant yellow colloid that was separated from the sediment by centrifuging at 9000rpm for 30min, and then dialyzed (3500Da) for 3days to remove the nitrate, yielding the CQDs.

For a typical procedure, 30mL thionyl chloride was mixed with the solid of as-made CQDs, and after ultrasonic treatment for 1h, the excess thionyl chloride was removed by evaporation. The acyl chloride derivative was stirred in a sealed flask with a series of organic amino groups including EDA, butanediamine (BDA), hexanediamine (HMDA), dodecylamine (DDA) or octadecylamine (ODA) in N2 atmosphere at 110°C, yielding a series of grafted CQDs, respectively, such as EDA-CQDs, BDA-CQDs, HMDA-CQDs, DDA-CQDs, and ODA-CQDs. The as-obtained hydrophilic CQDs, including EDA-CQDs, BDA-CQDs and HMDA-CQDs were vacuum-distilled to remove the solvents, then washed with ethanol several times, and finally vacuum-dried overnight. Besides, the DDA-CQDs and ODA-CQDs were purified by extraction with hot methanol in a Soxhlet apparatus [28].

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of the coal structures was performed by a QUANTA 450 microscope. The CQDs were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Cu Kα, λ =1.5406Å) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips Tecnai G2 20). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Thermofisher UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were scanned on a Bruker EQUINOX 55 and Thermo Scientific Nicolet iN10.

QY determination was achieved according to the previously established procedure. Rhodamine B (literature QY 0.31 at 340nm) was selected as a reference. The grafted CQDs samples such as EDA-CQDs, BDA-CQDs and HMDA-CQDs were dissolved in DI water, while the samples of DDA-CQDs and ODA-CQDs were dispersed in petroleum ether, respectively. The QYs were calculated by comparing the integrated PL intensity (excited at 340nm) and the absorbance value (at 340nm) of the standard with each of the coal-based CQDs, based on the following equation:
(1)
Φ =
Φ
r
×
A
r
I
r
×
I
A
×
n
2
n
r
2
where Φ is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index, and A is the optical density. The subscript “r” refers to the reference fluorophore of a known QY. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, absorbancies in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength of 340nm.

The morphology and structure of coal and CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . It can be clearly seen from SEM image of coal precursor (Fig. 2a) that Jincheng anthracite has irregular size and shape distributions, but has a relatively uniform structure. The XRD pattern of the carbon precursor shows a wide (002) diffraction peak at ca. 26° (Fig. 2b), suggesting a long-range disordered characteristic of the coal. After chemical oxidation, the CQDs with a uniform size and shape distribution are produced (TEM image, Fig. 2c). The Gaussian fitting curve (Fig. 2d) indicates that the pristine CQDs can be well-dispersed with an average size of 2.04nm. It is known that anthracite can be defined as a coal with high degree of coalification, and a number of small crystallites are involved within its matrix, which are easily exfoliated and grafted by oxidation treatment. Benefiting from the oxygen-related surface defects of the CQDs, which is evidenced by the wider (002) diffraction peak (Fig. 2b), the resultant CQDs possess a more disordered structure in comparison to the original coal. Moreover, It can be noted that no obvious changes are observed in the morphology and size distribution of the CQDs before and after EDA-grafting [33] (Fig. S1).

It is also noted that of the amine-grafted CQDs, the surface hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity performances vary with the length of hydrocarbon chains of organic amino molecules, thus leading to their high stabilization in the water or organic phase. The detailed results were shown in Figs. 1 and S2–S4. It is clearly seen that EDA-CQDs can disperse in water phase and bright blue fluorescence is emitted under UV light excitation (Fig. 1). This is also the case for BDA-CQDs and HMDA-CQDs (Figs. S2 and S3). It is very interesting that DDA-CQDs (Fig. 1) and ODA-CQDs (Fig. S4) disperse in petroleum ether, that is, hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity can be tuned and controlled by changing the surface characteristics of CQDs in the presence of organic amino molecules.

The optical features of the as-obtained CQDs were examined by UV–vis absorption and PL spectroscopy, of which the detailed results are shown in Fig. 3 . It is clearly seen that the UV–vis spectrum shows a strong peak at around 217nm that is attributed to π–π ∗ transition of an aromatic system [34]. An obvious peak at 273nm can also be observed in the PL excitation spectrum. Besides, other relatively weak shoulders at the wavelength of 306nm, 340nm, and 390nm are present. The CQDs show the maximum PL emission peak centered at 450nm with the excitation wavelength (273nm). The QY measured using rhodamine B as a standard is 0.73% at 340nm excitation, for the detailed information, please refer to Table 1 .

The chemical composition and surface states of the as-obtained CQDs and the grafted CQDs were characterized by the FTIR, of which the detailed results are shown in Fig. 4 . The CO, C–O and O–H vibration modes are common features of CQDs, indicating the presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups during the chemical oxidation [35]. Several bands from 2800 to 3000cm−1 are assigned to the vibration of long hydrocarbon [28]. Furthermore, the characteristic Amide I and Amide II bands at 1650 and 1550cm−1 provide a proof for covalent attachment of organic amino groups [28], demonstrating that the organic amine has been successfully grafted to CQDs via the amide bond. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy further confirms that the amine has been successfully grafted to CQDs, and the high ratio of nitrogen to oxygen are observed (Fig. S5 and Table S2).

To illustrate the absorption effect, the measured PL emission spectra of the original CQDs and the surface-modified ones are normalized by dividing their respective absorption coefficients at the wavelength of 360nm. Accordingly, the QY of CQDs could be evaluated in terms of the PL intensity shown in Fig. 5 . It is also found that the PL emission spectra of the modified CQDs with different organic agents show similar spectrographic features, though the PL emission peak shifts slightly from the wavelength of 427nm to 440nm, suggesting that they share the similar PL properties. Nevertheless, compared with the original CQDs, amine-grafted CQDs all show enhanced PL intensities to some extent. The corresponding QY demonstrates an amine-dependent behavior and varies with the hydrocarbon length of organic amines, and the detailed results are shown in Table 1. It is noted that the original CQDs with hydrophilicity only have the lowest QY which is 0.73%. After grafting with amines, the QY increases obviously and varies with the hydrocarbon length of organic amines and surface hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity. Of the grafted-CQDs, the EDA grafted CQDs have the highest QY value (18.6%), which is in agreement with that in literatures [36]. The reason behind the enhanced QY may be attributed to specific proton transfer to the conjugated fluorophore-like structure from amine ligands [33].

To further evaluate the optical properties of the as-prepared CQDs and the grafted CQDs, a more detailed PL study was carried out with different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6a, the as-prepared CQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent PL behavior. As the excitation wavelengths increase, the PL peak shifts to long wavelengths and its intensity decreases rapidly, being similar to most of the luminescent carbon nanoparticles [1,2]. The strongest excitation peak appears at the absorption band (Fig. 3), which may be attributed to the optical selection of the surface defect states near Fermi level of the CQDs. In contrast, the optical properties of the amine-grafted CQDs (Fig. 6b and f) are similar to that of the original ones. The PL emission spectra are still broad and excitation-dependent, nevertheless, the enhanced PL is still observed after grafting amines in comparison to the original CQDs (Figs. S6–S10).

Recently, luminescent CQDs are not uncommon to evaluate its potential as optical detectors by being dispersed in polymer matrixes such as gelatin matrices [31]. Here, the EDA-CQDs were mixed with the solution of gelatin at 50°C to make CQDs/gelatin hybrid monoliths under agitation without further chemical modification, in which the loading fraction of CQDs in gelatin can be easily controlled via changing the ratio of the EDA-CQDs in gelatin at room temperature. The images in Fig. 7 show the homogeneous dispersion of EDA-CQDs in gelatin matrices, and the monoliths are transparent and have a good optical quality. It is noted that when the concentration of CQDs ranges from 0.04 to 0.08wt.%, all of the monoliths show a remarkably blue fluorescence. However, a lower PL was observed under UV light excitation as the CQDs concentration further increases ranging from 0.08 to 0.4wt.%. The reason behind this is attributed to the concentration-induced quenching effect.

In summary, we have developed a facile one-step wet chemistry route to prepare highly luminescent CQDs from Jincheng anthracite, and surface grafting of the CQDs can be achieved by covalent grafting with a series of amine-grafted molecules to their surface. The fluorescent properties are dependent on their surface chemistry, and hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity can be tailored and controlled by changing the surface characteristics of CQDs in the presence of organic amino molecules. The as-synthesized CQDs grafted with EDA were found to emit a strong blue fluorescence with a maximum QY of 18.6% at the excitation of 340nm. All of the CQDs share the similar PL emission spectra, even though the QY varies with the grafted organic amine. However, the adjustment and control of fluorescence CQDs are still in the early stage, and the luminescent mechanism is not obvious. Optimization and mechanism studies concerning CQDs are underway.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1203292 and 21336001), the Education Department of Liaoning Province of China (T2013001), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (DUT14LAB06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.04.083.
